author: @VeniceInventors
text: My dad had a much smaller version of this, maybe 25cm long, meant for precision drilling so it had a small chuck at the end, and the lower hand-held cylinder was what spun the shaft so it didn't collapse/change length but would spin cw/ccw as the cylinder would be sled up and down. I really liked the way it smoothly rotated.

you could make the front mount a small hex bit holder (for tiny hex bits)..  and this might be a goo tool to take apart electronics...  maybe.. 
certainly more interesting than the small powered screwdrivers..  :-)  
I wonder if the original design managed 'torque out'  vs screwing in downwar pressure..

author: @jerrywhidby.
text:
Zachary T. Furbish of Augusta, Maine invented the spiral ratchet screwdriver that became known as the Yankee screwdriver. Furbish patented his invention on April 15, 1895. 
Explanation
The North Brothers Manufacturing Company of Philadelphia, Pennsylvania first marketed the Yankee screwdriver in 1895. The company was also known as North Bros. In 1946, Stanley acquired North Bros. and continued to produce many of their tools. 
Furbish brought his experience in tool design and patents for a spiral ratchet screwdriver to North Bros. when they purchased the Forest City Screwdriver Co. in 1897. Furbish went on to receive 30 patents for hand tools made by North Bros. 
The term "Yankee screwdriver" is often used to describe any push/pull type screwdriver, not just those made by North Bros. or Stanley.

author: @psocretes8183
text: I remember buying one of those in the seventies, I think it cost me £16. When expressing shock at the price the salesman said it will pay for itself in a day on price work.  In reality what we did was smash the screw most the the way in with a hammer and just finish it off with the Yankee.

replies.author: @JSfuckgoogle
replies.text: Hopefully you got fired, for hammering in SCREWS! 👀😔

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: the hammer is singly the most useful tool i have ever owned!

author: @billy-go9kx
text: My dad had one in 60's.  They were useful, but after time they were just a gimmick.  Only useful for very long screws.   Once some torque was needed it was awkward to control due to its length.

replies.author: @psocretes8183
replies.text: "Once some torque was needed it was awkward to control due to its length.", Not really, when it got too hard to screw in by pumping we would lock the mechanism with the ring lock, and that would allow you to finish off the screwing.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: Yankee screwdrivers were before rechargeable drill/drivers. They were awkward to use as they were too long and sometimes you ended  up skidding out of the screw head and damaging the surface you were working on.

replies.author: @psocretes8183
replies.text: @ I'm a retired carpenter I used them for more than half my life, they only jumped out and damaged the work if you weren't competent at using them. Like every tool it takes knowledge of the quirks.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: hmmm - i've used one for years - i find them good
